author: @stevecummins324
text:
much of the confusion over order of operations, happens because of the algorithms older/simpler calculators do sums... it's as if they add brackets around everything said before, and immediately after pressing say a + button...

using a partial example of *term rewriting* (a set of rules, which can be part of a formal grammar... where contents of brackets can be written as something else )  , such a calculator could be partially modelled as performing a sum  akin to the following

4+3*2+1
->(4+3*2+1)
->((4+3)*2+1)
->(7*2+1)
->((7*2)+1)
->(14+1)
->(15)
->15

with term rewriting that includes the correct order of operations (which are essential to keep maths consistent, and prevent daft results like 1=2)....

4+3*2+1
->(4+3*2+1)
->(4+(3*2)+1)

->(4+6+1)
->((4+6)+1)
->(10+1)
->(11)
->11

hope that helps some.

author: @stevecummins324
text: talking of symbols and abstractions....  a while ago I'd volunteered to attended a parenting course, and there we were , with our name and photos on little sticky  labels we had to wear due to being visitors in the building, and we were getting told labels are always bad. Put my hand up, and asked the lecturer, so what's a kid asking for when they point at a letter, an animal or tbh pretty much anything . Seems to me they're wanting to know what it's called, which also known as a label. the lecturer indicated they'd never thought about it that way before

replies.author: @a.j.atkinsonart1082
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes, but my point being ( no pun intended 🙂), just one example, a dot is not used as a decimal separator in all countries. Recent MIR tests on subjects whilst performing mathematical calculations have shown that all the types of mathematical reasoning used in the experiment activated different regions of the brain than the ones activated by verbal semantics and language processing. It's this that challenges the common refrain that math is a language. I suppose, one could conclude that when mentioning language in this context, its not just meant in the verbal sense, but in its broader sense, as you have clearly demonstrated. Sorry, I never intended to introduce a 'semantic rabbit hole', so to speak 🙄, just thought it was worth noting. Great work by the way 👍 Heres one to think about, propagating electrical energy from radio waves in space via a scalar wave transmission and reception in order for the recieved energy to greatly exceed the transmitted energy. This has been shown to happen with the use of Professor Meyl Konstantin's educational transmitter and reciever kit. Although these experiments were only harvesting energy from other man-made radio frequencies within our atmosphere,  imagine the possibilities of harvesting from space, ie, via a transmitter on the moon.

'King Charles laughed and joked, after his head was cut off' (His head = NOT King Charles's head)

Maths is INDEED a language. I totally agree.

author: @ecospider5
text: When tutoring I would encourage some students to think of math as 2 classes. How numbers are used to describe our real world. And then the language we use to communicate that to each other. So when teaching about circles and triangles we would play with circles and triangles for hours with no new words. Then I would teach the words like circumference diameter parallel separate after they already understand what those concepts are.

author: @ecospider5
text:
I am a natural math student. I have been taught very little math in all the math classes I have taken because the math it self seemed obvious to me. I learned how others wanted me to communicate that math to them. I was learning the language of math. The comparison of the outside of a circle to across a circle is always the same. Well of course. You want me to refer to that as circumference diameter and pi well I can do that. 

Great video. Excellent way of describing it.

author: @jsoswell
text: Thanks Robert! I wish more people understood abstract thinking in a world of set rules. Many seem to think that they don't need to know the rules. But, as I learned in music theory, you have to know the rules before you decide to break them, otherwise it just sounds like noise. Without clearly defined rules, chess would be unplayable, or just not fun at all. Because of the relational rules, one can start to see possibility of nearly infinite possibility. Without rules, there are no patterns. Without patterns, there is no pattern recognition. Without pattern recognition, there is no meaning. Without meaning, there is no purpose. I choose to live in a world where 2+2=4. Not because of any social construct, but because without those rules/principles, there is no beauty. Keep up the great thought work. Your videos inspire me.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I found what you said to be inspiring too mate and I couldn't agree with you more - people seem to think rules are something that binds you but in all honesty these rules are just agreements so we can communicate and create - in fact knowing them frees you

author: @cavelvlan25
text: 3:10 lost me

replies.author: @ecospider5
replies.text:
That is probably the hardest concept he described if you have not seen it before. In math when you see a letter where a number should be that letter is just a place holder. Sometimes we know what number that letter is supposed to represent sometimes we don’t. In this instance we know what numbers  the letters are supposed to represent.
Here are the 2 “math sentences”
2+2=4
A+B=C
Since we are saying both sentences are the same we know A is the same as 2. And B is the same as 2 and C is the same as 4. Or 
A=2
B=2
C=4

But this is only true because we said both sentences were the same. 

In a different math sentence, known as a math equation, The place holder A might not be 2. But in every math sentences 2 is always 2. 

Know I’m just rambling but here is the next level of this concept. You go to lunch with a friend. You eat $7 of food. The check arrives and is $10. So you do some subtraction in your head and know what your friend owes. This written in the language of math is. 
$10-$7= C
We are using C as the place holder for what your friend owes until we do the subtraction and find out. 
$3=C 
or
$3 is what your friend owes. Because we had decided to use C as the placeholder for your friend. 

In your head you would never replace you friend with C you would just think of them as your friend. But if we want to write down the math sentence writing your friend is to long. So we just replace those 2 words with C to make it easier to write. 

If this actually makes sense to you then congratulations. You are a month into pre-algebra.

replies.author: @saiello2061
replies.text: @ecospider5 It's more fundamental than that, there is nothing to "work out" here. The bald statement "A+B=C" is an axiom, i.e., it is an assertion ( that might form the beginning of an argument ) about the concept of numbers, the concept of operations on those number concepts and a statement about what the conceptual result will be. In this particular case, the only meaning that can be taken from it is: If you take the concept of ANY number that is represented by the symbol 'A' and the concept of any another number 'B' and you apply the concept of '+' ( however you have defined it to be ) to those two number concepts, you will get ANOTHER number concept 'C'. Its basically saying that if you '+' two numbers together you'll get another NUMBER concept and not the concept of a TREE. This kind of thing is hard for people to grasp because the philosophical aspect of maths is never taught. If maths teaching did start off this way then maybe people wouldn't have such a hard time with the weird symbols they encounter scrawled on the blackboard 😁.
